cross_connections.display_label: Third cousins served opposite sides of Civil War
cross_connections.notes: JSM enlisted Confederate Army May 1861 age 17; Bristol served as 1st Lieutenant in 29th Connecticut Volunteers (Colored), USCT, wounded at Chaffin's Farm Sep 29 1864. Common ancestors Jonathan Edwards Sr. and Sarah Pierpont Edwards; their grandfathers Jonathan Edwards Jr. and Pierpont Edwards were brothers.

units: Confederate Army (specific regiment not yet documented)
military_service.role: Enlisted in the Confederate Army May 11, 1861 at age seventeen, immediately after Fort Sumter. Specific regiment not yet documented; whether he was present at Chaffin's Farm on the Confederate side when his Union third cousin Eugene Stuart Bristol was wounded there Sep 29 1864 remains unknown.

narrative_blocks.header: Enlisted Confederate Army age seventeen, May 1861
narrative_blocks.body: John Samuel de Montmollin enlisted in the Confederate Army on May 11, 1861 at age seventeen, about a month after Fort Sumter, and served through the entire Civil War. His specific Confederate regiment is not yet documented in available sources. He returned home with his family's antebellum Savannah wealth largely dissipated by war's end.

narrative_blocks.header: Third cousin Bristol served on Union USCT side
narrative_blocks.body: John Samuel's third cousin Eugene Stuart Bristol (born 1843) served on the Union side as a First Lieutenant in the Twenty-ninth Connecticut Volunteers Colored — a United States Colored Troops regiment. Bristol was wounded at the Battle of Chaffin's Farm on September 29, 1864 in the Army of the James. Their common ancestors are Jonathan Edwards and Sarah Pierpont Edwards; their grandfathers Jonathan Edwards Jr. and Pierpont Edwards were brothers, making this one of the documented Hooker-descendant Civil-War-opposite-sides cousin pairings.

narrative_blocks.header: Two marriages, eleven children, Savannah-to-Florida arc
narrative_blocks.body: After his father's death, John Samuel de Montmollin returned from Switzerland to Savannah Georgia, and later lived in Palatka Florida with his widowed mother — the family wealth largely lost in and after the Civil War. He married twice (Sarah Ellen Hasson April 29, 1862 with seven children; Frances Eudor Hunnicutt September 15, 1892 with four children) — eleven children total. He died March 23, 1912 at age sixty-eight.

sources.short: Wikipedia; de Montmollin family genealogy; FamilySearch

narrative_blocks.header: Abolitionist's grandson became a slave trader
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather Jonathan Edwards Jr. was a Connecticut minister who preached against slavery in the 1790s. By the 1850s, John was brokering enslaved people in Savannah and operating a slave pen above his warehouse.

narrative_blocks.header: Sent the schooner Wanderer to the Congo
narrative_blocks.body: In 1858 his ship made an illegal voyage to West Africa and returned with four hundred captives delivered to coastal Georgia plantations. A federal grand jury declined to indict him in April 1859.

narrative_blocks.header: His slave pen became Savannah's first Black school
narrative_blocks.body: After Sherman took Savannah in December 1864, the U.S. government gave the warehouse to the city's African American community. On January 10, 1865, Black children marched from First African Baptist Church to the building and opened Bryan School there.

narrative_blocks.header: Killed in steamboat blast near his slave pen
narrative_blocks.body: On June 9, 1859 the J.G. Lawton's boiler exploded fourteen miles up the Savannah River. His body was found seventy yards away in the marsh, a handkerchief still gripped in his hand.

narrative_blocks.header: Will demanded education, sobriety, two foreign languages
narrative_blocks.body: He instructed his children to conduct themselves as Christians, learn French and Spanish fluently, and avoid liquor and tobacco. The will took ten years to settle in court.
